planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, humus-rich, moderately moist soil in partial to deep shade; button fern is unusually drought tolerant for a fern and prefers somewhat drier, rockier conditions than most shade ferns — it will not tolerate waterlogged soil.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, humus-rich, moderately moist soil in partial to deep shade — button fern from New Zealand is one of the most charming and distinctive small ferns in cultivation, producing graceful, arching, dark-stemmed fronds lined with small, round, button-like leaflets (pinnae) that give it a uniquely textured, decorative appearance quite unlike typical ferns. It is substantially more drought tolerant than most ferns, preferring rocky or well-drained, humus-rich soils rather than the constantly wet conditions needed by many shade ferns. This makes it an excellent choice for shaded container gardens, terrariums, hanging baskets, and rocky shade garden pockets where soil dries between waterings. It grows well as a houseplant in moderate to bright indirect light. In the Gulf Coast and South, it is hardy to around 25°F and grows outdoors year-round in zone 8–11 in partial to deep shade under trees and on north-facing banks.

planting.note: Plant divisions in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in deep shade; delta maidenhair requires consistently moist (never waterlogged) soil, high humidity, and shelter from wind and afternoon sun — dry air, cold drafts, and bright direct light will cause fronds to brown and die back rapidly.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to deep shade with high humidity — delta maidenhair is the most widely grown maidenhair fern, celebrated for its extraordinary, lacey delicacy: tiny, fan-shaped, bright green leaflets arranged in sprays on impossibly fine, wiry black stems that seem to float the foliage in mid-air. The fronds tremble with the slightest air movement, giving the plant an ethereal, shimmering quality. It demands consistent moisture, high humidity, and shelter from drying winds — the most common failure is under-watering or low humidity, which causes instant browning. Outdoors it is suitable only in zone 10–12 in sheltered, shaded, humid locations; in zone 8–9 it is an outstanding houseplant and covered-patio container plant. The classic indoor maidenhair; cultivars like 'Fragrantissimum', 'Fritz Lüthii', and 'Brilliant Else' are among the most refined forms. Mist frequently, water consistently, and keep away from heating vents and air conditioning.

planting.note: Plant divisions in moist, slightly alkaline to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Florida maidenhair fern grows naturally on shaded limestone outcrops and the bases of limestone walls and tree roots in Florida hammocks and the Caribbean, requiring good drainage and slightly alkaline to neutral (not acidic) conditions.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly alkaline to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to deep shade — Florida maidenhair fern is native to Florida hammock forests and limestone outcrops throughout Florida, the Caribbean, and tropical America, closely resembling the popular houseplant maidenhair (A. raddianum) but more cold and heat tolerant and uniquely adapted to the limestone-influenced, neutral to slightly alkaline soils of Florida garden settings. The fronds are more robust and somewhat less fragile than A. raddianum, making it more suitable as an outdoor landscape plant in zone 9–11. New fronds emerge with a lovely pinkish-bronze color that matures to bright green, creating a multicolored effect on established clumps. It is excellent for shaded patio containers, covered porches, and shaded garden beds with neutral to slightly alkaline limestone soils, where it provides an elegant, lacey texture. Unlike A. raddianum, it tolerates the outdoor humidity and heat of zone 9–10 without the constant intensive care needed by its more tropical relative.

planting.note: Plant divisions or transplants in moist, slightly alkaline to neutral, calcareous (limestone-based), humus-rich, very well-drained soil in deep shade; walking fern is highly specific in its substrate requirements — it grows naturally on shaded, moist, mossy limestone and calcareous rocks and will not thrive in acidic or poorly-drained soils.

directions: Plant in moist, calcareous, humus-rich, very well-drained soil on shaded limestone surfaces, moss-covered rocks, or alkaline woodland beds in deep shade — walking fern is a unique and fascinating native fern of the eastern US, producing long, narrow, tapering fronds with heart-shaped bases that extend to 12–18 inches and root at their tips wherever they contact moist substrate, slowly 'walking' outward to colonize a shaded limestone outcrop or mossy rock wall. The entire plant is distinctive and easily recognized: its uncut, lance-shaped fronds bear no resemblance to typical fernlike foliage. It is strictly associated with shaded, moist, mossy limestone and calcareous rock faces in the eastern deciduous forest, growing in deep shade of overhanging cliffs, ravine walls, and shaded limestone boulders. It is an outstanding plant for the specialized shaded rock garden with limestone substrate, adding a uniquely charming and ecologically interesting element to native wildflower rock gardens.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in fall or early spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately moist to dry, humus-rich soil in partial to deep shade; ebony spleenwort is one of the most adaptable and drought-tolerant of all native ferns, succeeding in both moist woodland and dry, rocky conditions in shade.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately moist to dry, humus-rich soil in partial to deep shade — ebony spleenwort is one of the most widely distributed and adaptable native ferns in North America, distinguished by its narrow, once-pinnate fronds on shiny, dark reddish-brown to black (ebony) stems that give it its common name. It grows in an extraordinary range of conditions: dry, rocky woodland slopes; bases of limestone outcrops; pine forest floors; moist ravine walls; and the dry shade under large trees where most ferns would fail. Unlike many ferns, it tolerates moderately dry, rocky conditions that defeat less adaptable species. The fronds are semi-evergreen in zone 7–9, providing year-round greenery in shaded gardens. It self-sows by spores to naturalize quietly in appropriate shaded, well-drained conditions, never becoming weedy. An outstanding low-maintenance fern for dry shade under trees in southern gardens.

planting.note: Plant divisions or transplants in spring in moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; American germander spreads by stolons in ideal conditions — it is most appropriate for naturalistic or wildlife plantings where spreading is welcome.

directions: Plant in moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade — American germander is a native perennial of moist woodland edges, wet meadows, roadsides, and stream margins throughout eastern North America, producing spikes of rose-pink to lavender tubular flowers with exserted stamens in summer that are excellent for native bees, bumble bees, and hummingbirds. The aromatic foliage has a sage-like scent when crushed (giving rise to the common name 'wood sage') and was used medicinally by indigenous peoples and early American herbalists. It spreads by stolons to form colonies in moist, shaded conditions — excellent for naturalizing along shaded stream banks, wet meadow edges, and the shaded margins of rain gardens. A relative of culinary germander (T. chamaedrys) and a historically used bitter herb for digestive complaints. Very adaptable and requires essentially no care once established in appropriate moist-to-moderate-moisture soil.

planting.note: Direct sow seed in fall or scarify and sow in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun to light shade; yellow wild indigo is slow to establish — it may take 3 years to bloom from seed — but is extremely long-lived and low-maintenance once established.

directions: Direct sow in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun — yellow wild indigo is the Gulf Coast and south-central species of Baptisia, producing upright spikes of clear, bright yellow pea-flowers in April–May on compact, mounded, blue-green foliage that remains attractive and deer-resistant all season after the flowers fade. It is native to the prairies and open woodlands of Texas, Louisiana, and Oklahoma, where it is a defining plant of the coastal prairie and post oak savanna flora. Like all false indigos, it develops a deep, extensive taproot that makes it highly drought tolerant and difficult to transplant once established — plant it in its permanent location from seed or small transplant and leave it undisturbed. The inflated, spherical seed pods are attractive in dried arrangements. Nitrogen-fixing; improves soil for neighboring plants. Slow to establish but essentially immortal once settled — clumps are known to persist and bloom for decades.

planting.note: Plant container specimens in fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun to partial shade; saucer magnolia blooms on bare wood in early spring and is susceptible to late frost damage to the buds — avoid planting in frost pockets or north-facing spots where late freezes are common.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade — saucer magnolia is one of the most spectacular flowering trees in the world for spring impact, producing enormous, tulip-shaped flowers (4–10 inches across) in shades ranging from deep rose-purple to pale pink to white, borne on bare branches before the leaves emerge in late winter to early spring. A single tree in bloom is visible from hundreds of feet away. The hybrid origin (M. denudata x M. liliiflora) combines cold hardiness and floriferous habit; named cultivars include 'Lennei' (deep rose-purple), 'Alexandrina' (pink and white), 'Brozzonii' (nearly white), and 'Rustica Rubra' (rich rose). The main risk in the South is late frost damage to early-opening buds in zone 5–7; in zone 8–9, this is rarely a problem and plants bloom reliably and abundantly. An outstanding focal point or specimen tree for the front landscape.

planting.note: Plant container specimens in fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to moderately moist, loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; Florida maple is significantly more heat, drought, and alkaline-soil tolerant than the northern sugar maple (Acer saccharum) and is the maple of choice for Gulf Coast gardens.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to moderately moist, loamy soil in full sun to partial shade — Florida maple is the southern adaptation of the sugar maple group, a medium-sized native tree of limestone bluffs, rich upland woods, and creek bottoms throughout the Southeast, providing outstanding fall color (orange, scarlet, and gold) in zone 7–9 where the northern sugar maple fails in summer heat. The distinctive 3-lobed leaves are smaller than those of northern sugar maple; trees are compact, multi-season ornamental trees with smooth gray bark, golden spring flower clusters, and the classic maple winged seeds (samaras) in spring. Florida maple tolerates the heat and soil alkalinity of zone 8–9 gardens significantly better than northern sugar maple cultivars and is the appropriate choice for fall color, shade, and wildlife value in Gulf Coast landscapes. Trees yield a small quantity of sap that can be made into a light maple syrup in the brief late-winter cold spells of zone 7–8.

planting.note: Direct sow seed in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to moderately moist, loamy to sandy soil in full sun; sundrops (unlike evening primrose) blooms by day and is an excellent day-blooming perennial for open sunny borders where evening primroses would go unnoticed at their peak.

directions: Direct sow in fall or transplant in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun — sundrops is a day-blooming native perennial (unlike its nocturnal cousin the evening primrose) that produces a long season of bright, clear, four-petaled lemon-yellow flowers 1.5–2 inches across from May through July on upright reddish stems with attractive, narrow, reddish-tinged leaves. The red pigmentation of the stems and foliage deepens through summer and turns spectacular bronzy-red in fall, providing excellent late-season color after the blooms end. It spreads by rhizomes and self-sowing to naturalize in open, sunny, dry areas, making it excellent for prairie gardens, wildflower meadows, and low-maintenance dry-slope plantings. The similar O. tetragona and its cultivar 'Fireworks' (with showy orange-red buds opening to yellow) are closely related and widely grown garden selections offering longer bloom periods. Drought tolerant; requires excellent drainage; no care once established.
